(**VGM**) is the [soundtrack](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack") that accompanies [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"). Early video game music was once limited to sounds of early [sound chips](/wiki/Sound_chips "Sound chips"), such as [programmable sound generators](/wiki/Programmable_sound_generator "Programmable sound generator") (PSG) or [FM synthesis](/wiki/FM_synthesis "FM synthesis") chips. **Video game music** (**VGM**) is the [soundtrack](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack") that accompanies [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"). Early video game music was once limited to sounds of early [sound chips](/wiki/Sound_chips "Sound chips"), such as [programmable sound generators](/wiki/Programmable_sound_generator "Programmable sound generator") (PSG) or [FM synthesis](/wiki/FM_synthesis "FM synthesis") chips. These limitations have led to the style of music known as [chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune"), which became the sound of the early video games.

With technological advances, video game music has grown to include a wider range of sounds. Players can hear music in video games over a game's title screen, menus, and gameplay.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Game soundtracks can also change depending on a player's actions or situation, such as indicating missed actions in [rhythm games](/wiki/Rhythm_game "Rhythm game"), informing the player they are in a dangerous situation, or rewarding them for specific achievements.

Video game music can be one of two kinds: original or [licensed](/wiki/Music_licensing "Music licensing").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

The popularity of video game music has created [education](/wiki/Education "Education") and job opportunities, generated awards, and led video game soundtracks to be commercially sold and performed in concerts.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Early video game technology and computer chip music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early video game technology and computer chip music")]

See also: [Chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune") and [Music Macro Language](/wiki/Music_Macro_Language "Music Macro Language")

At the time video games had emerged as a popular form of entertainment in the late 1970s, [music](/wiki/Music "Music") was stored on physical media in [analog](/wiki/Analog_recording "Analog recording") waveforms such as [cassette tapes](/wiki/Cassette_tape "Cassette tape") and [phonograph records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"). Such components were expensive and prone to breakage under heavy use, making them less than ideal for use in an [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet"), though in rare cases such as *[Journey](/wiki/Journey_(1983_video_game) "Journey (1983 video game)")*, they were used. A more affordable method of having music in a video game was to use digital means, where a specific computer chip would change electrical impulses from computer code into analog sound waves [on the fly](/wiki/On_the_fly "On the fly") for output on a speaker. Sound effects for the games were also generated in this fashion.

While this allowed for the inclusion of music in early [arcade video games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"), it was usually [monophonic](/wiki/Monophonic "Monophonic"), [looped](/wiki/Loop_(music) "Loop (music)") or used sparingly between stages or at the start of a new game, such as the [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco") titles *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")* (1980) composed by Toshio Kai or *[Pole Position](/wiki/Pole_Position "Pole Position")* (1982) composed by Nobuyuki Ohnogi.[[3]](#cite_note-vgmdb_namco-3) The first such game to use a continuous background [soundtrack](/wiki/Soundtrack "Soundtrack") was [Tomohiro Nishikado](/wiki/Tomohiro_Nishikado "Tomohiro Nishikado")'s *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, released by [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito") in 1978.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) It had four descending [chromatic](/wiki/Diatonic_and_chromatic "Diatonic and chromatic") [bass notes](/wiki/Bass_note "Bass note") repeating in a loop, though it was dynamic and interacted with the player, increasing pace as the enemies descended on the player.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The first video game to feature continuous, melodic [background music](/wiki/Background_music "Background music") was *[Rally-X](/wiki/Rally-X "Rally-X")*, released by Namco in 1980, featuring a simple tune that repeats continuously during [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay").[[6]](#cite_note-gradar2-6) The decision to include any music into a video game meant that at some point it would have to be transcribed into computer code. Some music was original, some was [public domain music](/wiki/Public_domain_music "Public domain music") such as folk songs. Sound capabilities were limited; the popular [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600") home system, for example, was capable of generating only two tones at a time.

As advances were made in silicon technology and costs fell, a definitively new generation of arcade machines and [home consoles](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(third_generation) "History of video game consoles (third generation)") allowed for great changes in accompanying music. In arcades, machines based on the [Motorola 68000](/wiki/Motorola_68000_series "Motorola 68000 series") CPU and accompanying various [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation") YM [programmable sound generator](/wiki/Programmable_sound_generator "Programmable sound generator") [sound chips](/wiki/Sound_chip "Sound chip") allowed for several more tones or "channels" of sound, sometimes eight or more. The earliest known example of this was [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s 1980 arcade game *[Carnival](/wiki/Carnival_(video_game) "Carnival (video game)")*, which used an [AY-3-8910](/wiki/AY-3-8910 "AY-3-8910") chip to create an electronic rendition of the [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") 1889 composition "[Over The Waves](/wiki/Over_The_Waves "Over The Waves")" by [Juventino Rosas](/wiki/Juventino_Rosas "Juventino Rosas").[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4)

[Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s 1981 arcade game *[Frogger](/wiki/Frogger "Frogger")* introduced a dynamic approach to video game music, using at least eleven different gameplay tracks, in addition to level-starting and [game over](/wiki/Game_over "Game over") themes, which change according to the player's actions. This was further improved upon by Namco's 1982 arcade game *[Dig Dug](/wiki/Dig_Dug "Dig Dug")*, where the music stopped when the player stopped moving.[[7]](#cite_note-7) *Dig Dug* was composed by Yuriko Keino, who also composed the music for other Namco games such as *[Xevious](/wiki/Xevious "Xevious")* (1982) and *[Phozon](/wiki/Phozon "Phozon")* (1983).[[3]](#cite_note-vgmdb_namco-3) [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s 1982 arcade game *[Super Locomotive](/wiki/Super_Locomotive "Super Locomotive")* featured a [chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune") rendition of [Yellow Magic Orchestra](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra "Yellow Magic Orchestra")'s "[Rydeen](/wiki/Solid_State_Survivor "Solid State Survivor")" (1979);[[8]](#cite_note-8) several later [computer games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") also [covered](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") the song, such as *Trooper Truck* (1983) by [Rabbit Software](/wiki/Rabbit_Software "Rabbit Software") as well as *[Daley Thompson's Decathlon](/wiki/Daley_Thompson%27s_Decathlon "Daley Thompson's Decathlon")* (1984) and *[Stryker's Run](/wiki/Stryker%27s_Run "Stryker's Run")* (1986) composed by [Martin Galway](/wiki/Martin_Galway "Martin Galway").[[9]](#cite_note-whosampled_covers-9)

Home console systems also had a comparable upgrade in sound ability beginning with the [ColecoVision](/wiki/ColecoVision "ColecoVision") in 1982 capable of four channels. However, more notable was the Japanese release of the [Famicom](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") in 1983 which was later released in the US as the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") in 1985. It was capable of five channels, one being capable of simple [PCM](/wiki/Pulse-code_modulation "Pulse-code modulation") sampled sound. The home computer [Commodore 64](/wiki/Commodore_64 "Commodore 64") released in 1982 was capable of early forms of filtering effects, different types of [waveforms](/wiki/Waveform "Waveform") and eventually the undocumented ability to play 4-bit samples on a pseudo fourth sound channel. Its comparatively low cost made it a popular alternative to other home computers, as well as its ability to use a TV for an affordable display monitor.

Early games used simple tone generation and/or [frequency modulation synthesis](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis") to simulate instruments for melodies, and used a "noise channel" for simulating percussive noises. PCM samples were limited to short sound bites (*Monopoly*), used in place of percussion sounds (*[Super Mario Bros. 3](/wiki/Super_Mario_Bros._3 "Super Mario Bros. 3")*) or used for basslines (*[Gimmick!](/wiki/Gimmick! "Gimmick!")*). Home consoles often shared music channels with sound effects. For example, a spaceship's 1400 Hz square wave laser beam sound effect would interrupt any music on that channel.

The mid-to-late 1980s software releases for these platforms had music developed by more people with greater musical experience than before. Quality of composition improved noticeably, and evidence of the popularity of the music of this time period remains even today. Composers who made a name for themselves with their software include [Koichi Sugiyama](/wiki/Koichi_Sugiyama "Koichi Sugiyama") (*[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")*),[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Nobuo Uematsu](/wiki/Nobuo_Uematsu "Nobuo Uematsu") (*[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*), [Rob Hubbard](/wiki/Rob_Hubbard "Rob Hubbard") (*[Monty on the Run](/wiki/Monty_on_the_Run "Monty on the Run")*, *[International Karate](/wiki/International_Karate "International Karate")*), [Koji Kondo](/wiki/Koji_Kondo "Koji Kondo") (*[Super Mario Bros.](/wiki/Super_Mario_Bros. "Super Mario Bros.")*, *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (video game)")*), [Miki Higashino](/wiki/Miki_Higashino "Miki Higashino") (*[Gradius](/wiki/Gradius "Gradius")*, *[Yie Ar Kung-Fu](/wiki/Yie_Ar_Kung-Fu "Yie Ar Kung-Fu")*, *[Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles](/wiki/Teenage_Mutant_Ninja_Turtles_(arcade_game) "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles (arcade game)")*), [Hiroshi Kawaguchi](/wiki/Hiroshi_Kawaguchi_(composer) "Hiroshi Kawaguchi (composer)") (*[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")*, *[Hang-On](/wiki/Hang-On "Hang-On")*, *[Out Run](/wiki/Out_Run "Out Run")*), [Hirokazu Tanaka](/wiki/Hirokazu_Tanaka "Hirokazu Tanaka") (*[Metroid](/wiki/Metroid "Metroid")*, *[Kid Icarus](/wiki/Kid_Icarus "Kid Icarus")*, *[EarthBound](/wiki/EarthBound "EarthBound")*), [Martin Galway](/wiki/Martin_Galway "Martin Galway") (*[Daley Thompson's Decathlon](/wiki/Daley_Thompson%27s_Decathlon "Daley Thompson's Decathlon")*, *[Stryker's Run](/wiki/Stryker%27s_Run "Stryker's Run")*, *[Times of Lore](/wiki/Times_of_Lore "Times of Lore")*), [David Wise](/wiki/David_Wise_(composer) "David Wise (composer)") (*[Donkey Kong Country](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_Country "Donkey Kong Country")*), [Yuzo Koshiro](/wiki/Yuzo_Koshiro "Yuzo Koshiro") (*[Dragon Slayer](/wiki/Dragon_Slayer_(series) "Dragon Slayer (series)")*, *[Ys](/wiki/Ys_(series) "Ys (series)")*, *[Shinobi](/wiki/Shinobi_(series) "Shinobi (series)")*, *[ActRaiser](/wiki/ActRaiser "ActRaiser")*, *[Streets of Rage](/wiki/Streets_of_Rage "Streets of Rage")*), Mieko Ishikawa (*Dragon Slayer*, *Ys*), and [Ryu Umemoto](/wiki/Ryu_Umemoto "Ryu Umemoto") ([visual novels](/wiki/Visual_novel "Visual novel"), [shoot 'em ups](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up")). By the late 1980s, video game music was being sold as cassette tape soundtracks in Japan, inspiring American companies such as [Sierra](/wiki/Sierra_Entertainment "Sierra Entertainment"), [Cinemaware](/wiki/Cinemaware "Cinemaware") and [Interplay](/wiki/Interplay_Entertainment "Interplay Entertainment") to give more serious attention to video game music by 1988.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [Golden Joystick Awards](/wiki/Golden_Joystick_Award "Golden Joystick Award") introduced a category for *Best Soundtrack of the Year* in 1986, won by *[Sanxion](/wiki/Sanxion "Sanxion")*.

Some games for cartridge systems have been sold with extra audio hardware on board, including *[Pitfall II](/wiki/Pitfall_II "Pitfall II")* for the Atari 2600 and several late Famicom titles. These chips add to the existing sound capabilities.

### Early digital synthesis and sampling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early digital synthesis and sampling")]

See also: [Chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune")

From around 1980, some arcade games began taking steps toward digitized, or [sampled](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing) "Sampling (signal processing)"), sounds. Namco's 1980 arcade game *[Rally-X](/wiki/Rally-X "Rally-X")* was the first known game to use a [digital-to-analog converter](/wiki/Digital-to-analog_converter "Digital-to-analog converter") (DAC) to produce sampled tones instead of a tone generator.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) That same year, the first known video game to feature [speech synthesis](/wiki/Speech_synthesis "Speech synthesis") was also released: Sunsoft's [shoot 'em up](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") game *[Stratovox](/wiki/Stratovox "Stratovox")*.[[6]](#cite_note-gradar2-6) Around the same time,[[12]](#cite_note-karen_10-1-12) the introduction of [frequency modulation synthesis](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis") ([FM synthesis](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis")), first commercially released by [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation") for their [digital synthesizers](/wiki/Digital_synthesizer "Digital synthesizer") and FM [sound chips](/wiki/Sound_chip "Sound chip"), allowed the tones to be manipulated to have different sound characteristics, where before the tone generated by the chip was limited to the design of the chip itself. [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s 1983 arcade game *[Gyruss](/wiki/Gyruss "Gyruss")* used five square wave[[13]](#cite_note-13) sound chips along with a DAC, which were used to create an electronic version of [J. S. Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach")'s *[Toccata and Fugue in D minor](/wiki/Toccata_and_Fugue_in_D_minor,_BWV_565 "Toccata and Fugue in D minor, BWV 565")*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Beyond arcade games, significant improvements to [personal computer game](/wiki/Personal_computer_game "Personal computer game") music were made possible with the introduction of [digital](/wiki/Digital_synthesizer "Digital synthesizer") FM [synth boards](/wiki/Sound_card "Sound card"), which [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation") released for Japanese computers such as the [NEC PC-8801](/wiki/NEC_PC-8801 "NEC PC-8801") and [PC-9801](/wiki/NEC_PC-9801 "NEC PC-9801") in the early 1980s, and by the mid-1980s, the PC-8801 and [FM-7](/wiki/FM-7 "FM-7") had built-in FM sound. The sound FM synth boards produced are described as "warm and pleasant sound". Musicians such as [Yuzo Koshiro](/wiki/Yuzo_Koshiro "Yuzo Koshiro") and [Takeshi Abo](/wiki/Takeshi_Abo "Takeshi Abo") utilized to produce music that is still highly regarded within the [chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune") community.[[15]](#cite_note-hg101_retro-15) The widespread adoption of [FM synthesis](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis") by consoles would later be one of the major advances of the [16-bit era](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)"), by which time 16-bit arcade machines were using multiple FM synthesis chips.[[12]](#cite_note-karen_10-1-12)

One of the earliest [home computers](/wiki/Home_computer "Home computer") to make use of digital signal processing in the form of sampling was the [Amiga](/wiki/Amiga "Amiga") in 1985. The computer's sound chip featured four independent 8-bit [digital-to-analog converters](/wiki/Digital-to-analog_converter "Digital-to-analog converter"). Developers could use this platform to take [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(signal_processing) "Sampling (signal processing)") of a music performance, sometimes just a single note long, and play it back through the computer's [sound chip](/wiki/Sound_chip "Sound chip") from memory. This differed from *Rally-X* in that its hardware DAC was used to play back simple [waveform](/wiki/Waveform "Waveform") samples, and a sampled sound allowed for a complexity and authenticity of a real instrument that an FM simulation could not offer. For its role in being one of the first and affordable, the Amiga would remain a staple tool of early [sequenced](/wiki/Music_sequencer "Music sequencer") music composing, especially in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Macintosh](/wiki/Mac_(computer) "Mac (computer)") and Amiga home computers offered these features before most others. The [Atari ST](/wiki/Atari_ST "Atari ST"), the Amiga's main rival, used a [Yamaha YM2149](/wiki/Yamaha_YM2149 "Yamaha YM2149") [Programmable Sound Generator](/wiki/Programmable_Sound_Generator "Programmable Sound Generator") (PSG); but, compared to Amiga's proprietary sound engine, Atari's PSG could handle only one channel of sound and needed the computer's CPU to process data. This was impractical until the 1989 release of the [Atari STE](/wiki/Atari_STE "Atari STE"), which used DMA techniques to play back PCM samples at up to 50 kHz. The ST remained relevant, however; equipped with a [MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI") controller and external ports, it became the choice for many professional musicians as a MIDI programming device.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[IBM PC clones](/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") in 1985 would not see any significant development in multimedia abilities for a few more years, and sampling would not become popular in other video game systems for several years. Though sampling had the potential to produce much more realistic sounds, each sample required much more data in [memory](/wiki/Computer_memory "Computer memory"). This was at a time when all memory, solid-state ([ROM cartridge](/wiki/ROM_cartridge "ROM cartridge")), magnetic ([floppy disk](/wiki/Floppy_disk "Floppy disk")) or otherwise was still very costly per [kilobyte](/wiki/Kilobyte "Kilobyte"). Sequenced sound chip-generated music, on the other hand, was generated with a few lines of comparatively simple code and took up far less precious memory.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Arcade systems pushed game music forward in 1984 with the introduction of FM (Frequency Modulation) synthesis, providing more organic sounds than previous PSGs. The first such game, *[Marble Madness](/wiki/Marble_Madness "Marble Madness")* used the Yamaha YM2151 FM synthesis chip.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

As home consoles moved into the [fourth generation](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)"), or 16-bit era, the hybrid approach (sampled and tone) to music composing continued to be used. The [Sega Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis") offered advanced graphics over the [NES](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") and improved sound synthesis features (also using a Yamaha chip, the [YM2612](/wiki/Yamaha_YM2612 "Yamaha YM2612")),[[17]](#cite_note-17) but largely held the same approach to sound design. 6 channels of FM along with 4 PSG channels add up to ten channels in total for tone generation, with the 6th FM channel swappable for PCM samples were available in [stereo](/wiki/Stereo "Stereo") instead of the NES's five channels in mono, one for PCM. As before, it was often used for percussion samples. The Genesis did not support 16-bit sampled sounds. Despite the additional tone channels, writing music still posed a challenge to traditional composers and it forced much more imaginative use of the [FM synthesizer](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis") to create an enjoyable listening experience. The composer Yuzo Koshiro utilized the Genesis hardware effectively to produce "[progressive](/wiki/Progressive_electronic_dance_music "Progressive electronic dance music"), catchy, [techno](/wiki/Techno "Techno")-style compositions far more advanced than what players were used to" for games such as *[The Revenge of Shinobi](/wiki/The_Revenge_of_Shinobi_(1989_video_game) "The Revenge of Shinobi (1989 video game)")* (1989) and the *[Streets of Rage](/wiki/Streets_of_Rage "Streets of Rage")* series, setting a "new high watermark for what music in games could sound like."[[18]](#cite_note-18) The soundtrack for *[Streets of Rage 2](/wiki/Streets_of_Rage_2 "Streets of Rage 2")* (1992) in particular is considered "revolutionary" and "ahead of its time" for [its blend](/wiki/Electro_house "Electro house") of [house music](/wiki/House_music "House music") with "[dirty](/wiki/Electro_house "Electro house")" [electro](/wiki/Electro_(music) "Electro (music)") basslines and "[trancey](/wiki/Trance_music "Trance music") electronic textures" that "would feel as comfortable in a [nightclub](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") as a video game."[[19]](#cite_note-19) Another important FM synth composer was [Ryu Umemoto](/wiki/Ryu_Umemoto "Ryu Umemoto"), who composed music for many [visual novels](/wiki/Visual_novel "Visual novel") and [shoot 'em ups](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") for PCs during the 1990s.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

As the cost of magnetic memory declined in the form of [diskettes](/wiki/Diskettes "Diskettes"), the evolution of video game music on the Amiga, and some years later game music development in general, shifted to [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") in some form. It took some years before Amiga game designers learned to wholly use digitized sound effects in music (an early exception case was the title music of [text adventure](/wiki/Interactive_fiction "Interactive fiction") game *[The Pawn](/wiki/The_Pawn_(video_game) "The Pawn (video game)")*, 1986). By this time, computer and game music had already begun to form its own identity, and thus many music makers intentionally tried to produce music that sounded like that heard on the [Commodore 64](/wiki/Commodore_64 "Commodore 64") and NES, which resulted in the [chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune") genre.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The release of a freely-distributed Amiga program named [Soundtracker](/wiki/Ultimate_Soundtracker "Ultimate Soundtracker") by Karsten Obarski in 1987 started the era of [MOD](/wiki/MOD_(file_format) "MOD (file format)")-format which made it easy for anyone to produce music based on digitized samples. [Module files](/wiki/Module_file "Module file") were made with programs called "[trackers](/wiki/Tracker_(music_software) "Tracker (music software)")" after Obarski's Soundtracker. This MOD/tracker tradition continued with PC computers in the 1990s. Examples of [Amiga games](/wiki/Amiga_games "Amiga games") using digitized instrument samples include [David Whittaker](/wiki/David_Whittaker_(video_game_composer) "David Whittaker (video game composer)")'s soundtrack for *[Shadow of the Beast](/wiki/Shadow_of_the_Beast_(1989_video_game) "Shadow of the Beast (1989 video game)")*, [Chris Hülsbeck](/wiki/Chris_H%C3%BClsbeck "Chris Hülsbeck")'s soundtrack for *[Turrican 2](/wiki/Turrican "Turrican")* and Matt Furniss's tunes for *[Laser Squad](/wiki/Laser_Squad "Laser Squad")*. [Richard Joseph](/wiki/Richard_Joseph "Richard Joseph") also composed some theme songs featuring vocals and lyrics for games by [Sensible Software](/wiki/Sensible_Software "Sensible Software") most famous being *[Cannon Fodder](/wiki/Cannon_Fodder_(video_game) "Cannon Fodder (video game)")* (1993) with a song "War Has Never Been So Much Fun" and *[Sensible World of Soccer](/wiki/Sensible_Soccer "Sensible Soccer")* (1994) with a song "Goal Scoring Superstar Hero". These songs used long vocal samples.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A similar approach to sound and music developments had become common in the arcades by this time and had been used in many [arcade system boards](/wiki/Arcade_system_board "Arcade system board") since the mid-1980s.[[21]](#cite_note-21) This was further popularized in the early 1990s by games like *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")* (1991) on the [CPS-1](/wiki/CP_System "CP System"), which used voice samples extensively along with sampled sound effects and percussion. [Neo Geo](/wiki/Neo_Geo "Neo Geo")'s MVS system also carried powerful sound development which often included [surround sound](/wiki/Surround_sound "Surround sound").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/SNES-Mod1-Console-Set.jpg/250px-SNES-Mod1-Console-Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:SNES-Mod1-Console-Set.jpg)

The [Super NES](/wiki/Super_Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Super Nintendo Entertainment System") (1991) brought digitized sound to console games.

The evolution also carried into home console video games, such as the release of the [Super Famicom](/wiki/Super_Famicom "Super Famicom") in 1990, and its US/EU version Super NES in 1991. It sported a specialized custom [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") chip for both the sound generation and for special hardware DSP. It was capable of eight channels of sampled sounds at up to 16-bit resolution, had a wide selection of DSP effects, including a type of [ADSR](/wiki/Attack-decay-sustain-release_envelope "Attack-decay-sustain-release envelope") usually seen in high-end synthesizers of the time, and full stereo sound. This allowed experimentation with applied acoustics in video games, such as musical acoustics (early games like *[Super Castlevania IV](/wiki/Super_Castlevania_IV "Super Castlevania IV")*, *[F-Zero](/wiki/F-Zero_(video_game) "F-Zero (video game)")*, *[Final Fantasy IV](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_IV "Final Fantasy IV")*, *[Gradius III](/wiki/Gradius_III "Gradius III")*, and later games like *[Chrono Trigger](/wiki/Chrono_Trigger "Chrono Trigger")*), directional (*[Star Fox](/wiki/Star_Fox_(1993_video_game) "Star Fox (1993 video game)")*) and spatial acoustics ([Dolby Pro Logic](/wiki/Dolby_Pro_Logic "Dolby Pro Logic") was used in some games, like *King Arthur's World* and *Jurassic Park*), as well as environmental and [architectural acoustics](/wiki/Architectural_acoustics "Architectural acoustics") (*[A Link to the Past](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda:_A_Link_to_the_Past "The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past")*, *[Secret of Evermore](/wiki/Secret_of_Evermore "Secret of Evermore")*). Many games also made heavy use of the high-quality sample playback capabilities (*[Super Star Wars](/wiki/Super_Star_Wars "Super Star Wars")*, *[Tales of Phantasia](/wiki/Tales_of_Phantasia "Tales of Phantasia")*). The only real limitation to this powerful setup was the still-costly [solid state memory](/wiki/Semiconductor_memory "Semiconductor memory"). Other consoles of the generation could boast similar abilities yet did not have the same circulation levels as the Super NES. The [Neo Geo](/wiki/Neo_Geo "Neo Geo") home system was capable of the same powerful sample processing as its arcade counterpart but was several times the cost of a Super NES. The [Sega CD](/wiki/Sega_CD "Sega CD") (the Mega CD outside North America) hardware upgrade to the Mega Drive (Genesis in the US) offered multiple PCM channels, but they were often passed over instead to use its capabilities with the CD-ROM itself.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The popularity of the Super NES and its software remained limited to regions where [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC") television was the broadcast standard. Partly because of the difference in frame rates of [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") broadcast equipment, many titles released were never redesigned to play appropriately and ran much slower than had been intended, or were never released. This showed a divergence in popular video game music between PAL and NTSC countries that still shows to this day. This divergence would be lessened as the fifth generation of home consoles launched globally, and as Commodore began to take a back seat to general-purpose PCs and Macs for developing and gaming.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Though the Mega CD/Sega CD, and to a greater extent the [PC Engine](/wiki/PC_Engine "PC Engine") in Japan, would give gamers a preview of the direction video game music would take in [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") music, the use of both sampled and sequenced music continues in game consoles even today. The huge data storage benefit of optical media would be coupled with progressively more powerful audio generation hardware and higher quality samples in the [Fifth Generation](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fifth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fifth generation)"). In 1994, the CD-ROM equipped [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") supported 24 channels of 16-bit samples of up to 44.1 kHz sample rate, samples equal to CD audio in quality. It also sported a few hardware DSP effects like [reverb](/wiki/Reverberation "Reverberation"). Many [Square](/wiki/Square_(video_game_company) "Square (video game company)") titles continued to use sequenced music, such as *[Final Fantasy VII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VII "Final Fantasy VII")*, *[Legend of Mana](/wiki/Legend_of_Mana "Legend of Mana")*, and *[Final Fantasy Tactics](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_Tactics "Final Fantasy Tactics")*. The [Sega Saturn](/wiki/Sega_Saturn "Sega Saturn") also with a CD drive supported 32 channels of PCM at the same resolution as the original PlayStation. In 1996, the [Nintendo 64](/wiki/Nintendo_64 "Nintendo 64"), still using a solid-state cartridge, actually supported an integrated and scalable sound system that was potentially capable of 100 channels of PCM, and an improved sample rate of 48 kHz. Games for the N64, because of the cost of the solid-state memory, typically had samples of lesser quality than the other two, however, and music tended to be simpler in construct.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The more dominant approach for games based on CDs, however, was shifting toward [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") audio.

### MIDI on the PC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: MIDI on the PC")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/IBM_PC_5150.jpg/250px-IBM_PC_5150.jpg)](/wiki/File:IBM_PC_5150.jpg)

The first developers of [IBM PC](/wiki/IBM_PC "IBM PC") computers neglected audio capabilities (first IBM model, 1981).



A music track from [3D Movie Maker](/wiki/3D_Movie_Maker "3D Movie Maker") made using [General MIDI](/wiki/General_MIDI "General MIDI")

In the same timeframe of the late 1980s to mid-1990s, the IBM PC clones using the [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") architecture became more ubiquitous, yet had a very different path in sound design than other PCs and consoles. Early PC gaming was limited to the [PC speaker](/wiki/PC_speaker "PC speaker"), and some proprietary standards such as the [IBM PCjr](/wiki/IBM_PCjr "IBM PCjr") 3-voice chip. While sampled sound could be achieved on the PC speaker using pulse width modulation, doing so required a significant proportion of the available processor power, rendering its use in games rare.

With the increase of x86 PCs in the market, there was a vacuum in sound performance in home computing that expansion cards attempted to fill. The first two recognizable standards were the [Roland MT-32](/wiki/Roland_MT-32 "Roland MT-32"), followed by the [AdLib](/wiki/AdLib "AdLib") sound card. Roland's solution was driven by [MIDI](/wiki/MIDI "MIDI") sequencing using advanced LA synthesizers. This made it the first choice for game developers to produce upon, but its higher cost as an end-user solution made it prohibitive. The AdLib used a low-cost FM synthesis chip from [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation"), and many boards could operate compatibly using the MIDI standard.

The [AdLib](/wiki/AdLib "AdLib") card was usurped in 1989 by [Creative](/wiki/Creative_Technology "Creative Technology")'s [Sound Blaster](/wiki/Sound_Blaster "Sound Blaster"), which used the same Yamaha FM chip in the AdLib, for compatibility, but also added 8-bit 22.05 kHz (later 44.1 kHz) digital audio recording and playback of a single stereo channel. As an affordable end-user product, the Sound Blaster constituted the core sound technology of the early 1990s; a combination of a simple FM engine that supported MIDI, and a DAC engine of one or more streams. Only a minority of developers ever used Amiga-style tracker formats in commercial PC games, (*[Unreal](/wiki/Unreal_(1998_video_game) "Unreal (1998 video game)")*) typically preferring to use the MT-32 or AdLib/SB-compatible devices. As general purpose PCs using x86 became more ubiquitous than the other PC platforms, developers drew their focus towards that platform.

The last major development before streaming music came in 1992: [Roland Corporation](/wiki/Roland_Corporation "Roland Corporation") released the first [General MIDI](/wiki/General_MIDI "General MIDI") card, the [sample-based](/wiki/Sample-based_synthesis "Sample-based synthesis") [SCC-1](/wiki/SCC-1 "SCC-1"), an add-in card version of the [SC-55](/wiki/SC-55 "SC-55") desktop MIDI module. The comparative quality of the samples spurred similar offerings from Soundblaster, but costs for both products were still high. Both companies offered 'daughterboards' with sample-based synthesizers that could be later added to a less expensive soundcard (which only had a DAC and a MIDI controller) to give it the features of a fully integrated card.

Unlike the standards of [Amiga](/wiki/Amiga "Amiga") or [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari"), a PC using x86 even then could be using a broad mix of hardware. Developers increasingly used MIDI sequences: instead of writing soundtrack data for each type of soundcard, they generally wrote a fully featured data set for the Roland application that would be compatible with lesser featured equipment so long as it had a MIDI controller to run the sequence. However, different products used different sounds attached to their MIDI controllers. Some tied into the Yamaha FM chip to simulate instruments, some daughterboards of samples had very different sound qualities; meaning that no single sequence performance would be accurate to every other General MIDI device.

All of these considerations in the products reflected the high cost of memory storage which rapidly declined with the optical CD format.

### Pre-recorded and streaming music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pre-recorded and streaming music")]

Main article: [Streaming audio in video games](/wiki/Streaming_audio_in_video_games "Streaming audio in video games")

Taking entirely pre-recorded music had many advantages over sequencing for sound quality. Music could be produced freely with any kind and number of instruments, allowing developers to simply record one track to be played back during the game. Quality was only limited by the effort put into mastering the track itself. Memory space costs that was previously a concern was somewhat addressed with optical media becoming the dominant media for software games. [CD quality](/wiki/CD_quality "CD quality") audio allowed for music and voice that had the potential to be truly indistinguishable from any other source or genre of music.

In fourth generation home video games and PCs this was limited to playing a [Mixed Mode CD](/wiki/Mixed_Mode_CD "Mixed Mode CD") audio track from a [CD](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") while the game was in play (such as *[Sonic CD](/wiki/Sonic_CD "Sonic CD")*). The earliest examples of Mixed Mode CD audio in video games include the [TurboGrafx-CD](/wiki/TurboGrafx-CD "TurboGrafx-CD") [RPG](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") franchises *[Tengai Makyō](/wiki/Tengai_Maky%C5%8D "Tengai Makyō")*, composed by [Ryuichi Sakamoto](/wiki/Ryuichi_Sakamoto "Ryuichi Sakamoto") from 1989,[[22]](#cite_note-hg101_tengai-22) and the [*Ys* series](/wiki/Ys_(series) "Ys (series)"), composed by Yuzo Koshiro and [Mieko Ishikawa](/w/index.php?title=Mieko_Ishikawa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mieko Ishikawa (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%9F%B3%E5%B7%9D%E4%B8%89%E6%81%B5%E5%AD%90 "jp:石川三恵子")] and arranged by [Ryo Yonemitsu](/w/index.php?title=Ryo_Yonemitsu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ryo Yonemitsu (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B1%B3%E5%85%89%E4%BA%AE "jp:米光亮")] in 1989. The *Ys* soundtracks, particularly *[Ys I & II](/wiki/Ys_I_%26_II "Ys I & II")* (1989), are still regarded as some of the most influential video game music ever composed.[[23]](#cite_note-kalata_ys-23)[[24]](#cite_note-rpgfan_falcom-24)[[25]](#cite_note-greening_kotowski-25)

However, there were several disadvantages of regular CD-audio. Optical drive technology was still limited in spindle speed, so playing an audio track from the game CD meant that the system could not access data again until it stopped the track from playing. [Looping](/wiki/Music_loop "Music loop"), the most common form of game music, was also a problem as when the laser reached the end of a track, it had to move itself back to the beginning to start reading again causing an audible gap in playback.

To address these drawbacks, some PC game developers designed their own [container formats](/wiki/Container_format "Container format") in house, for each application in some cases, to stream compressed audio. This would cut back on memory used for music on the CD, allowed for much lower latency and seek time when finding and starting to play music, and also allowed for much smoother looping due to being able to [buffer](/wiki/Data_buffer "Data buffer") the data. A minor drawback was that use of compressed audio meant it had to be decompressed which put load on the CPU of a system. As computing power increased, this load became minimal, and in some cases, dedicated chips in a computer (such as a sound card) would actually handle all the decompressing.

Fifth generation home console systems also developed specialised streaming formats and containers for compressed audio playback. Games would take full advantage of this ability, sometimes with highly praised results (*[Castlevania: Symphony of the Night](/wiki/Castlevania:_Symphony_of_the_Night "Castlevania: Symphony of the Night")*). Games ported from arcade machines, which continued to use FM synthesis, often saw superior pre-recorded music streams on their home console counterparts (*[Street Fighter Alpha 2](/wiki/Street_Fighter_Alpha_2 "Street Fighter Alpha 2")*). Even though the game systems were capable of "CD quality" sound, these compressed audio tracks were not true "CD quality." Many of them had lower sampling rates, but not so significant that most consumers would notice. Using a compressed stream allowed game designers to play back streamed music and still be able to access other data on the disc without interruption of the music, at the cost of CPU power used to render the audio stream. Manipulating the stream any further would require a far more significant level of CPU power available in the 5th generation.

Some games, such as the [*Wipeout*](/wiki/Wipeout_(video_game_series) "Wipeout (video game series)") series, continued to use full Mixed Mode CD audio for their soundtracks.

This overall freedom offered to music composers gave video game music the equal footing with other popular music it had lacked. A musician could now, with no need to learn about programming or the game architecture itself, independently produce the music to their satisfaction. This flexibility would be exercised as popular mainstream musicians would be using their talents for video games specifically. An early example is *Way of the Warrior* on the [3DO](/wiki/3DO "3DO"), with music by [White Zombie](/wiki/White_Zombie_(band) "White Zombie (band)"). A more well-known example is [Trent Reznor](/wiki/Trent_Reznor "Trent Reznor")'s score for *[Quake](/wiki/Quake_(video_game) "Quake (video game)")*.

An alternate approach, as with the *TMNT* arcade, was to take pre-existing music not written exclusively for the game and use it in the game. The game *[Star Wars: X-Wing vs. TIE Fighter](/wiki/Star_Wars:_X-Wing_vs._TIE_Fighter "Star Wars: X-Wing vs. TIE Fighter")* and subsequent [*Star Wars* games](/wiki/Star_Wars_video_games "Star Wars video games") took music composed by [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams "John Williams") for the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* films of the 1970s and 1980s and used it for the game soundtracks.

Both using new music streams made specifically for the game, and using previously released/recorded music streams are common approaches for developing sound tracks to this day. It is common for X-games sports-based video games to come with some popular artists recent releases (*SSX*, *Tony Hawk*, *Initial D*), as well as any game with heavy cultural demographic theme that has tie-in to music (*[Need For Speed: Underground](/wiki/Need_For_Speed:_Underground "Need For Speed: Underground")*, *[Gran Turismo](/wiki/Gran_Turismo_(series) "Gran Turismo (series)")*, and *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")*). Sometimes a hybrid of the two are used, such as in *[Dance Dance Revolution](/wiki/Dance_Dance_Revolution "Dance Dance Revolution")*.

Many sports game series like *[Madden NFL](/wiki/Madden_NFL "Madden NFL")*, *[NBA 2K](/wiki/NBA_2K "NBA 2K")*, and *[FIFA](/wiki/FIFA_(video_game_series) "FIFA (video game series)")* use popular and underground songs in their soundtrack to give their menus atmosphere. The phrase "FIFA song" has become popular in recent years, it describes a song (often not American) that is upbeat and has lots of rhythm. The inclusion on the *FIFA* soundtrack has given many artists exposure that helped launch their music careers.

Sequencing samples continue to be used in modern gaming where fully recorded audio is not viable. Until the mid-2000s, many larger games on home consoles used sequenced audio to save space. Additionally, most games on the [Game Boy Advance](/wiki/Game_Boy_Advance "Game Boy Advance") and [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") used sequenced music due to storage limitations. Sometimes a cross between sequencing samples, and streaming music is used. Games such as *[Republic: The Revolution](/wiki/Republic:_The_Revolution "Republic: The Revolution")* (music composed by [James Hannigan](/wiki/James_Hannigan "James Hannigan")[[26]](#cite_note-26)) and *[Command & Conquer: Generals](/wiki/Command_%26_Conquer:_Generals "Command & Conquer: Generals")* (music composed by Bill Brown) have utilised sophisticated systems governing the flow of incidental music by stringing together short phrases based on the action on screen and the player's most recent choices (see [dynamic music](/wiki/Dynamic_music "Dynamic music")). Other games dynamically mixed the sound on the game based on cues of the game environment.

As processing power increased dramatically in the 6th generation of home consoles, it became possible to apply special effects in realtime to streamed audio. In *[SSX](/wiki/SSX "SSX")*, if a snowboarder takes to the air after jumping from a ramp, the music softens or muffles a bit, and the ambient noise of wind and air blowing becomes louder to emphasize being airborne. When the snowboarder lands, the music resumes regular playback until its next "cue". The [LucasArts](/wiki/LucasArts "LucasArts") company pioneered this interactive music technique with their [iMUSE](/wiki/IMUSE "IMUSE") system, used in their early adventure games and the *Star Wars* flight simulators *[Star Wars: X-Wing](/wiki/Star_Wars:_X-Wing_(video_game) "Star Wars: X-Wing (video game)")* and *[Star Wars: TIE Fighter](/wiki/Star_Wars:_TIE_Fighter "Star Wars: TIE Fighter")*. Action games such as these will change dynamically to match the amount of danger. Stealth-based games will sometimes rely on such music, either by handling streams differently, or dynamically changing the composition of a sequenced soundtrack.

### Personalized soundtracks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personalized soundtracks")]

Being able to play one's own music during a game in the past usually meant turning down the game audio and using an alternative music player. Some early exceptions were possible on PC/Windows gaming in which it was possible to independently adjust game audio while playing music with a separate program running in the background. Some PC games, such as *[Quake](/wiki/Quake_(video_game) "Quake (video game)")*, play music from the CD while retrieving game data exclusively from the hard disk, thereby allowing the game CD to be swapped for any music CD. The first PC game to introduce in-game support for custom soundtracks was Lionhead Studio's *[Black & White](/wiki/Black_%26_White_(video_game) "Black & White (video game)")*. The 2001 game included an in-game interface for [Winamp](/wiki/Winamp "Winamp") that enabled the players to play audio tracks from their own playlists. In addition, this would sometimes trigger various reactions from the player's Creature, like dancing or laughing.

Some [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") games supported this by swapping the game CD with a music CD, although when the game needed data, players had to swap the CDs again. One of the earliest games, *[Ridge Racer](/wiki/Ridge_Racer_(video_game) "Ridge Racer (video game)")*, was loaded entirely into RAM, letting the player insert a music CD to provide a soundtrack throughout the entirety of the gameplay. In *[Vib Ribbon](/wiki/Vib_Ribbon "Vib Ribbon")*, this became a gameplay feature, with the game generating levels based entirely on the music on whatever CD the player inserted.

Microsoft's [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)") allowed music to be copied from a CD onto its internal hard drive, to be used as a "Custom Soundtrack", if enabled by the game developer. The feature carried over into the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360") where it became supported by the system software and could be enabled at any point. The [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") is also able to play custom soundtracks if it is enabled by the game (*[Excite Truck](/wiki/Excite_Truck "Excite Truck")*,[[27]](#cite_note-27) *[Endless Ocean](/wiki/Endless_Ocean "Endless Ocean")*[[28]](#cite_note-28)). The [PlayStation Portable](/wiki/PlayStation_Portable "PlayStation Portable") can, in games like *[Need for Speed Carbon: Own the City](/wiki/Need_for_Speed_Carbon:_Own_the_City "Need for Speed Carbon: Own the City")* and *[FIFA 08](/wiki/FIFA_08 "FIFA 08")*, play music from a [Memory Stick](/wiki/Memory_Stick "Memory Stick").

The [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") has the ability to utilize custom soundtracks in games using music saved on the hard drive, however few game developers used this function. *MLB 08: The Show*, released in 2008, has a My MLB sound track feature that allows the user to play music tracks of their choice saved on the hard drive of their PS3, rather than the preprogrammed tracks incorporated into the game by the developer. An update to *[Wipeout HD](/wiki/Wipeout_HD "Wipeout HD")*, released on the PlayStation Network, was made to also incorporate this feature.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In the video game *[Audiosurf](/wiki/Audiosurf "Audiosurf")*, custom soundtracks are the main aspect of the game. Users have to pick a music file to be analyzed. The game will generate a race track based on tempo, pitch and complexity of the sound. The user will then race on this track, synchronized with the music.

Games in the *Grand Theft Auto* series have supported custom soundtracks, using them as a separate in-game radio station. The feature was primarily exclusive to [PC](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") versions, and was adopted to a limited degree on console platforms. On a PC, inserting custom music into the stations is done by placing music files into a designated folder. For the Xbox version, a CD must be installed into the console's hard drive. For the iPhone version of *[Grand Theft Auto: Chinatown Wars](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto:_Chinatown_Wars "Grand Theft Auto: Chinatown Wars")*, players create an iTunes playlist which is then played by the game.

*[Forza Horizon 3](/wiki/Forza_Horizon_3 "Forza Horizon 3")* used a similar technology of custom soundtracks with the help of [Groove Music](/wiki/Groove_Music "Groove Music").
