The [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_network "Xbox network") supports [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital") software, sampling and playback rate of 16-bit @ 48 kHz (internal; with 24-bit hardware D/A converters), hardware codec streaming, and potential of 256 audio simultaneous channels.[[30]](#cite_note-30) PCs continue to rely on third-party devices for in-game sound reproduction, and SoundBlaster is largely the only major player in the entertainment audio expansion card business.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") handles multiple types of surround sound technology, including [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD") and [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio"), with up to [7.1](/wiki/7.1 "7.1") channels, and with sampling rates of up to 192 kHz.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Nintendo's [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") console shares many audio components with the [GameCube](/wiki/GameCube "GameCube") from the previous generation, including [Dolby Pro Logic II](/wiki/Dolby_Pro_Logic_II "Dolby Pro Logic II"). These features are extensions of technology already currently in use.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Impact and importance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Impact and importance")]

Many video game players believe that music can enhance game play and outlets such as *[Popular Science](/wiki/Popular_Science "Popular Science")* have stated that it is designed to "simultaneously stimulate your senses and blend into the background of your brain, because that's the point of the soundtrack. It has to engage you, the player, in a task without distracting from it. In fact, the best music would actually direct the listener to the task."[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Sound effects within game play are also believed to impact game performance. Ambient sounds such as those present in *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil")* are seen to enhance the tension felt by players, something that GameSpot stated was also used in cinema.[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33) Speeding up the sound effects and music in games such as *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* is also stated to have a strong impact on the gaming experience when done properly.[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33) Properly done, this can help create realism within virtuality and alert players to important scenes and information.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Music and sound effects can become memorable, enabling people to instantly recognize music or sound effects as well as hum or mimic the tune or sound effect.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36) Polygon has stated that despite the popularity of video game music, people may not always know the name of the composer.[[36]](#cite_note-:5-36)

## Licensing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Licensing")]

Using licensed music for video games became more popular as the medium used to distribute games grew large enough to accommodate songs alongside a game's other assets. Additionally, with the large growth of the video game market in the 2000s, song licensing became a lucrative route for music rights holders to gain part of that revenue. Games like those in the *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")* series became showcases of licensed music.[[37]](#cite_note-gibiz_license-37) [Music licensing](/wiki/Music_licensing "Music licensing") is generally complicated due to various copyright laws, typically with at least two separate copyrights to consider: the underlying composition's and the sound recording's. Most large video game developers and publishers who use licensed music typically have staff proficient in licensing to clear songs for use in video games with the various music labels and other creative persons.[[37]](#cite_note-gibiz_license-37)

Games with licensed music can have problems well past release if perpetual rights for the music are not secured for the game. Early games before the onset of [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") would have perpetual right for the music since there was no practical way to update the game following release at retail to deal with curtailed rights. However, digital distribution platforms, like [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"), [Xbox Live](/wiki/Xbox_Live "Xbox Live"), and [PlayStation Network](/wiki/PlayStation_Network "PlayStation Network") keep games up-to-date automatically. Music licenses for games sold through digital distribution may include limited terms, requiring the publisher to re-negotiate rights with the music's owner, or otherwise the music must be removed from the game through these updates.[[37]](#cite_note-gibiz_license-37) Notably, *[Alan Wake](/wiki/Alan_Wake "Alan Wake")* by [Remedy Entertainment](/wiki/Remedy_Entertainment "Remedy Entertainment"), first released in 2010, had to be pulled from digital sale in 2017 due to expiring music rights.[[38]](#cite_note-38) However, with Microsoft's help, Remedy was able to re-secure these rights a year later and returned the game for sale.[[39]](#cite_note-39) *[Alpha Protocol](/wiki/Alpha_Protocol "Alpha Protocol")* by [Obsidian Entertainment](/wiki/Obsidian_Entertainment "Obsidian Entertainment") was also pulled from sale in 2019 due to expiring music license rights, though there are no known plans if publisher [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") will seek to renew these.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Due to the [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act"), most of the popular video sites use automated forms of detection (like [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube")'s [Content ID](/wiki/Content_ID "Content ID")), flagging or blocking content that uses copyrighted music. To help players avoid this problem when streaming videos (see [Let's Play](/wiki/Let%27s_Play "Let's Play")), some games offer a "stream-safe" option, either disabling the music or replacing it with copyright-free or royalty-free music.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Game music as a genre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Game music as a genre")]

See also: [Chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune")

Many games for the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") and other early game consoles feature a similar style of musical composition that is sometimes described as the "video game genre." Some aspects of this style continue to influence certain music today, though gamers do not associate many modern game soundtracks with the older style. The genre's compositional elements largely developed due to technological restraints, while also being influenced by [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") bands, particularly [Yellow Magic Orchestra](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra "Yellow Magic Orchestra") (YMO), who were popular during the late 1970s to 1980s.[[43]](#cite_note-japantimes-43) YMO sampled sounds from several [classic arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") in their early albums, most notably *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* in the 1978 hit song "[Computer Game](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra_(album) "Yellow Magic Orchestra (album)")".[[44]](#cite_note-wire_1996-44) In turn, the band would have a major influence on much of the video game music produced during the [8-bit](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(third_generation) "History of video game consoles (third generation)") and [16-bit eras](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)").[[43]](#cite_note-japantimes-43)

Features of the video game music genre include:

* Pieces designed to repeat indefinitely, rather than having an arranged ending or fading out (they however create an atmosphere, especially in important scenes of the game. They introduce a philosophical dimension in the game, as they may introduce questioning in the mind of players, in relationship with their next action).
* Pieces lacking lyrics and playing over gameplay sounds.
* Limited polyphony. Only three notes can be played simultaneously on the Nintendo Entertainment System. A great deal of effort was put into composition to create the illusion of more notes playing at once.

Although the tones featured in [NES](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") music can be thought of as emulating a traditional four-piece rock band (triangle wave used as a bass, two pulse waves analogous to two guitars, and a white noise channel used for drums), composers would often go out of their way to compose complex and rapid sequences of notes, in part due to the restrictions mentioned above.[[45]](#cite_note-Collins_2008_p._25-45)[[46]](#cite_note-The_Disco_King_2010-46)[[47]](#cite_note-Carle_2005-47) This is similar to music composition during the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") period, when composers, particularly when creating solo pieces, focused on musical embellishments to compensate for instruments such as the [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord") that do not allow for expressive dynamics. For the same reason, many early compositions also feature a distinct jazz influence. These would overlap with later influences from [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") and [J-pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop") music, resulting in an equally distinct compositional style in the 16-bit era.

In an unrelated but parallel course in the European and North American developer scene, similar limitations were driving the musical style of [home computer games](/wiki/Home_computer_games "Home computer games"). [Module file](/wiki/Module_file "Module file") format music, particularly [MOD](/wiki/MOD_(file_format) "MOD (file format)"), used similar techniques but was more heavily influenced by the electronic music scene as it developed, and resulted in another very distinct subgenre. [Demos](/wiki/Demo_(computer_programming)#Music "Demo (computer programming)") and the developing [demoscene](/wiki/Demoscene "Demoscene") played a big part in the early years, and still influence video game music today.

As technological limitations gradually lifted, composers were given more freedom and, with the advent of CD-ROM, pre-recorded soundtracks came to dominate, resulting in a noticeable shift in composition and voicing style.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Popular early CD-ROM titles were released with high-resolution graphics and recorded music. Since the audio was not reliant on a sound-card's synthesis, CD-ROM technology ensured that composers and sound designers could know what audio would sound like on most consumer configurations and could also record sound effects, live instruments, vocals, and in-game dialogue.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Outside video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Outside video games")]

See also: [Chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune")

Appreciation for video game music is strong among fans and composers, particularly for music from the [third](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(third_generation) "History of video game consoles (third generation)") and [fourth](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)") generations of home video game consoles, and sometimes newer generations. This appreciation has been shown outside the context of a video game, in the form of CDs, sheet music, public performances, art installations, and popular music.

### CDs and sheet music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: CDs and sheet music")]

Selling video game soundtracks separately as CDs has become increasingly popular in the industry.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) Interpretive albums, remixes, and live performance albums were also common variations to original soundtracks (OSTs).[[50]](#cite_note-:4-50)

[Koichi Sugiyama](/wiki/Koichi_Sugiyama "Koichi Sugiyama") was an early figure in this practice, and following the release of the first *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Warrior "Dragon Warrior")* game in 1986, a live performance CD of his compositions was released and performed by the Tokyo Strings Ensemble (then later by other groups including the [London Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/London_Philharmonic_Orchestra "London Philharmonic Orchestra"), and [Tokyo Metropolitan Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Symphony_Orchestra "Tokyo Metropolitan Symphony Orchestra")).

By 1987, [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") were selling 50,000 to 100,000 game soundtrack CDs annually.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Yuzo Koshiro, another early figure, released a live performance of the *[Actraiser](/wiki/Actraiser "Actraiser")* soundtrack. Both Koshiro's and fellow [Falcom](/wiki/Falcom "Falcom") composer Mieko Ishikawa's contributions to *[Ys](/wiki/Ys_(video_game) "Ys (video game)")* music would have such long-lasting impact that there were more albums released of Ys music than of almost all other game-type music.

Like [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") soundtracks, these soundtracks and even sheet music books were usually marketed exclusively in Japan. Therefore, interested gamers outside Japan had to import the soundtracks and/or sheet music books through on or offline firms specifically dedicated to video game soundtrack imports. This has been somewhat less of an issue more recently as domestic publishers of anime and video games have been producing western equivalent versions of the OSTs for sale in UK and US, though these are often for more popular titles. Video game music companies like Materia Collective have pursued and produced published book editions of video game music.

The sale of video game soundtracks has created a growing symbiotic relationship between the music industry and the games industry.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) Commonly, games are being used to promote and sell licensed music, rather than just original score, and recording artists are being used to market and sell games.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) Music marketing agency Electric Artists conducted a study that revealed a number of interesting statistics surrounding "hard-core gamers" and their music habits: 40% of hard-core gamers bought the CD after hearing a song they liked in a video game, 73% of gamers said soundtracks within games help sell more CDs, and 40% of respondents said a game introduced them to a new band or song, then 27% of them went out and bought what they heard.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4) Some games' soundtracks have become so popular they have reached platinum status, such as *[NBA Live 2003](/wiki/NBA_Live_2003 "NBA Live 2003")*.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4)

### Public performance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Public performance")]

Many original composers have publicly exhibited their music through symphonic concert performances. Once again, Koichi Sugiyama was the first to execute this practice in 1987 with his "Family Classic Concert" featuring live performances of *Dragon Quest* music and had continued these concert performances almost annually. In 1991, he also formed a series called Orchestral Game Music Concerts, notable for featuring music of other talented game composers such as [Yoko Kanno](/wiki/Yoko_Kanno "Yoko Kanno") (*Nobunaga's Ambition*, *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*, *Uncharted Waters*), [Nobuo Uematsu](/wiki/Nobuo_Uematsu "Nobuo Uematsu") (*[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*), Keiichi Suzuki (*Mother/Earthbound*), and Kentaro Haneda (*Wizardry*).[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Following suit, compositions by Nobuo Uematsu on *[Final Fantasy IV](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_IV "Final Fantasy IV")* were arranged into *[Final Fantasy IV: Celtic Moon](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_IV:_Celtic_Moon "Final Fantasy IV: Celtic Moon")*, a live performance by string musicians with strong Celtic influence recorded in Ireland. The Love Theme from the same game has been used as an instructional piece of music in Japanese schools.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

With the success of [Square](/wiki/Square_(video_game_company) "Square (video game company)")'s 1990s games *[Final Fantasy VI](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VI "Final Fantasy VI"), [Final Fantasy VII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VII "Final Fantasy VII")* and *[Final Fantasy VIII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VIII "Final Fantasy VIII")* by Nobuo Uematsu, and *[Chrono Trigger](/wiki/Chrono_Trigger "Chrono Trigger"), [Xenogears](/wiki/Xenogears "Xenogears")* and *[Chrono Cross](/wiki/Chrono_Cross "Chrono Cross")* by [Yasunori Mitsuda](/wiki/Yasunori_Mitsuda "Yasunori Mitsuda"), public performance began to gain international popularity. On August 20, 2003, music written for video games such as *Final Fantasy* and *The Legend of Zelda* was performed for the first time outside Japan, by the [Czech National Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Czech_National_Symphony_Orchestra "Czech National Symphony Orchestra") in a [Symphonic Game Music Concert](/wiki/Symphonic_Game_Music_Concert "Symphonic Game Music Concert") in Leipzig, Germany at the Gewandhaus concert hall.[[50]](#cite_note-:4-50) This event was held as the official opening ceremony of Europe's biggest trading fair for video games, the GC Games Convention and repeated in 2004, 2005, 2006 and 2007.[[50]](#cite_note-:4-50)

On November 17, 2003, Square Enix launched the *Final Fantasy Radio* on [America Online](/wiki/America_Online "America Online"). The radio station has initially featured complete tracks from *[Final Fantasy XI](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XI "Final Fantasy XI")* and *[Final Fantasy XI: Rise of Zilart](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XI "Final Fantasy XI")* and samplings from *Final Fantasy VII* through *[Final Fantasy X](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_X "Final Fantasy X")*.[[50]](#cite_note-:4-50)

The first officially sanctioned [Final Fantasy concert](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_Music_Concert "Final Fantasy Music Concert") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") was performed by the [Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra") at [Walt Disney Concert Hall](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Concert_Hall "Walt Disney Concert Hall") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), on May 10, 2004.[[54]](#cite_note-54) All seats at the concert were sold out in a single day. "Dear Friends: Music from Final Fantasy" followed and was performed at various cities across the United States. Nobuo Uematsu has also performed a variety of Final Fantasy compositions live with his rock band, [The Black Mages](/wiki/The_Black_Mages "The Black Mages").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

On July 6, 2005, the [Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Los Angeles Philharmonic Orchestra") also held a [Video Games Live](/wiki/Video_Games_Live "Video Games Live") concert at the [Hollywood Bowl](/wiki/Hollywood_Bowl "Hollywood Bowl"), an event founded by video game music composers [Tommy Tallarico](/wiki/Tommy_Tallarico "Tommy Tallarico") and [Jack Wall](/wiki/Jack_Wall_(composer) "Jack Wall (composer)").[[50]](#cite_note-:4-50) This concert featured a variety of video game music, ranging from *Pong* to *[Halo 2](/wiki/Halo_2 "Halo 2")*. It also incorporated real-time video feeds that were in sync with the music, as well as laser and light special effects. Media outside the video game industry, such as [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") and *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, have covered their subsequent world tours.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

On August 20, 2006, the Malmö Symphonic Orchestra with host [Orvar Säfström](/wiki/Orvar_S%C3%A4fstr%C3%B6m "Orvar Säfström") performed the outdoor game music concert Joystick in [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") before an audience of 17,000, holding the current record of attendance for a game music concert.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Säfström has since continued to produce game music concerts around Europe under the names Joystick and Score.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

From April 20–27, 2007, [Eminence Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Eminence_Symphony_Orchestra "Eminence Symphony Orchestra"), an orchestra dedicated to video game and anime music, performed the first part of their annual tour, the "A Night in Fantasia" concert series in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). Whilst Eminence had performed video game music as part of their concerts since their inception, the 2007 concert marked the first time ever that the entire setlist was pieces from video games. Up to seven of the world's most famous game composers were also in attendance as special guests. Music performed included Red Alert 3 Theme: Soviet March by James Hannigan and [Shadow of the Colossus](/wiki/Shadow_of_the_Colossus "Shadow of the Colossus") by [Kow Otani](/wiki/Kow_Otani "Kow Otani").

Since 2010, video games-themed "[pops](/wiki/Pops_orchestra "Pops orchestra")" concerts have become a major proportion of the revenue in many United States [concert halls](/wiki/List_of_concert_halls "List of concert halls"), as traditional classical music performances decline in popularity.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

On March 16, 2012, the [Smithsonian American Art Museum](/wiki/Smithsonian_American_Art_Museum "Smithsonian American Art Museum")'s "The Art of Video Games" exhibit opened featuring a chipmusic soundtrack at the entrance by artists [8 Bit Weapon](/wiki/8_Bit_Weapon "8 Bit Weapon") & [ComputeHer](/wiki/ComputeHer "ComputeHer").[[61]](#cite_note-61) 8 Bit Weapon also created a track called "The art of Video Games Anthem" for the exhibit as well.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

The first video game music-focused concert for the [BBC Proms](/wiki/BBC_Proms "BBC Proms") was held on August 1, 2022.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") will launch the BAFTA Games in Concert tour in 2026, featuring music from games that have won [BAFTA Game Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Games_Awards "British Academy Games Awards"), with [Austin Wintory](/wiki/Austin_Wintory "Austin Wintory") compositing and conducting the live orchestra. Initial tour dates will take place across United Kingdom with plans for international venues later.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### In popular music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: In popular music")]

See also: [Chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune"), [Bitpop](/wiki/Bitpop "Bitpop"), and [Nintendocore](/wiki/Nintendocore "Nintendocore")

In the [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") industry, video game music and sounds have appeared in songs by various popular artists.[[65]](#cite_note-Puls-65) Arcade game sounds had a particularly strong influence on the [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"),[[66]](#cite_note-66) [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") (particularly [synthpop](/wiki/Synthpop "Synthpop"))[[67]](#cite_note-sfweekly_interview-67) and [electro music](/wiki/Electro_music "Electro music")[[68]](#cite_note-allmusic_electro-68) genres during the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") in the early 1980s. Arcade game sounds had an influence on synthpop pioneers [Yellow Magic Orchestra](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra "Yellow Magic Orchestra"),[[69]](#cite_note-guardian_ymo-69) who sampled *Space Invaders* sounds in their influential 1978 debut album, particularly the hit song "[Computer Game](/wiki/Yellow_Magic_Orchestra_(album) "Yellow Magic Orchestra (album)")".[[44]](#cite_note-wire_1996-44) In turn, the band would have a major influence on much of the video game music produced during the [8-bit](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(third_generation) "History of video game consoles (third generation)") and [16-bit eras](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fourth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fourth generation)").[[43]](#cite_note-japantimes-43)

Other pop songs based on *Space Invaders* soon followed, including "Disco Space Invaders" (1979) by Funny Stuff,[[70]](#cite_note-wire_2002-70) "Space Invaders" (1980) by Playback,[[71]](#cite_note-71) and the hit songs "[Space Invader](/wiki/Pretenders_(album) "Pretenders (album)")" (1980) by [The Pretenders](/wiki/The_Pretenders "The Pretenders")[[70]](#cite_note-wire_2002-70) and "Space Invaders" (1980) by Uncle Vic.[[72]](#cite_note-72) [Buckner & Garcia](/wiki/Buckner_%26_Garcia "Buckner & Garcia") produced a successful album dedicated to video game music in 1982, *[Pac-Man Fever](/wiki/Pac-Man_Fever_(album) "Pac-Man Fever (album)")*.[[73]](#cite_note-timemag-73) Former YMO member [Haruomi Hosono](/wiki/Haruomi_Hosono "Haruomi Hosono") also released a 1984 album produced entirely from Namco [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") samples entitled *Video Game Music*, an early example of a [chiptune](/wiki/Chiptune "Chiptune") record[[74]](#cite_note-74) and the first video game music album.[[75]](#cite_note-75) [Warp](/wiki/Warp_(record_label) "Warp (record label)")'s record "Testone" (1990) by [Sweet Exorcist](/wiki/Sweet_Exorcist_(band) "Sweet Exorcist (band)") sampled video game sounds from YMO's "Computer Game" and defined Sheffield's [bleep techno](/wiki/Yorkshire_Bleeps_and_Bass "Yorkshire Bleeps and Bass") scene in the early 1990s.[[76]](#cite_note-sicko_brewster_76-76) In 1991, American [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") band [Pixies](/wiki/Pixies_(band) "Pixies (band)") released a cover version of the main theme from the arcade game *[Narc](/wiki/Narc_(video_game) "Narc (video game)")* as a B-side to the single "[Planet of Sound](/wiki/Planet_of_Sound "Planet of Sound")".[[77]](#cite_note-77)

More recently, "video game beats" have appeared in popular songs such as [Kesha](/wiki/Kesha "Kesha")'s "[Tik Tok](/wiki/Tik_Tok_(song) "Tik Tok (song)")",[[65]](#cite_note-Puls-65) the [best-selling single](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_singles "List of best-selling singles") of 2010,[[78]](#cite_note-ifpi10-78) as well as "U Should Know Better" by [Robyn](/wiki/Robyn "Robyn") featuring [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg"),[[65]](#cite_note-Puls-65) and "Hellbound" by [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"). The influence of video game music can also be seen in contemporary [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica") music by artists such as [Dizzee Rascal](/wiki/Dizzee_Rascal "Dizzee Rascal") and [Kieran Hebden](/wiki/Kieran_Hebden "Kieran Hebden").[[69]](#cite_note-guardian_ymo-69) [Grime](/wiki/Grime_music "Grime music") music in particular samples [sawtooth wave](/wiki/Sawtooth_wave "Sawtooth wave") sounds from video games which were popular in [East London.](/wiki/East_(London_sub_region) "East (London sub region)")[[79]](#cite_note-79) English [power metal](/wiki/Power_metal "Power metal") band [DragonForce](/wiki/DragonForce "DragonForce") is also known for their "retro video game influenced" sound.

## Video game music education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Video game music education")]

Video game music has become part of the curriculum at the degree, undergraduate, and graduate levels in many traditional colleges and universities.[[80]](#cite_note-80) According to the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association"), there are over 400 schools offering courses and degrees in video game design in the United States, many of which include sound and music design.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) [Berklee College of Music](/wiki/Berklee_College_of_Music "Berklee College of Music"), [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), and the [New England Conservatory](/wiki/New_England_Conservatory "New England Conservatory") have all introduced game music into their music programs. These programs offer immersive education in music composition, orchestration, editing and production.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Other post-secondary schools have more games-focused programs, such as [DigiPen Institute of Technology](/wiki/DigiPen_Institute_of_Technology "DigiPen Institute of Technology"), [Columbia College Chicago](/wiki/Columbia_College_Chicago "Columbia College Chicago"), and [Academy of Art University](/wiki/Academy_of_Art_University "Academy of Art University"),[[84]](#cite_note-84) who all offer programs in Music and Sound Design.[[85]](#cite_note-85) These programs include courses in sound effect creation, interactive sound design, and scripting music.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Similar programs have gained popularity in Europe. The [Utrecht School of the Arts](/wiki/Utrecht_School_of_the_Arts "Utrecht School of the Arts") (Faculty of Art, Media and Technology) has offered a Game Sound and Music Design program since 2003. The [University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire") has a program in Music Composition and Technology for Film and Games, [Leeds Beckett University](/wiki/Leeds_Beckett_University "Leeds Beckett University") offers Sound and Music for Interactive Games, and dBs Music Bristol teaches Sound for Games and Apps.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

More informal institutions, like the training seminars at [GameSoundCon](/wiki/GameSoundCon "GameSoundCon") also feature classes in how to compose video game music.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Extracurricular organizations devoted to the performance of video game music have also been implemented in tandem with these new curriculum programs. The [Gamer Symphony Orchestra at the University of Maryland](/wiki/Gamer_Symphony_Orchestra_at_the_University_of_Maryland "Gamer Symphony Orchestra at the University of Maryland") performs self-arranged video game music and the [Video Game Orchestra](/wiki/Video_Game_Orchestra "Video Game Orchestra") is a semiprofessional outgrowth of students from the Berklee College of Music and other Boston-area schools.[[91]](#cite_note-:3-91)

According to the [National Association for Music Education](/wiki/National_Association_for_Music_Education "National Association for Music Education"), video game music is now being taught at elementary and secondary school levels to aid in the understanding of music composition.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Students at [Magruder High School](/wiki/Colonel_Zadok_A._Magruder_High_School "Colonel Zadok A. Magruder High School") in [Montgomery County](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland"), [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") have even started a student-run gamer orchestra, and many high school bands perform game music.[[91]](#cite_note-:3-91)

## Academic study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Academic study")]

Main article: [Ludomusicology](/wiki/Ludomusicology "Ludomusicology")

Academic research on video game music began in the late 1990s,[[93]](#cite_note-93) and developed through the mid-2000s. Early research on the topic often involved historical studies of game music, or comparative studies of video game music and film music (see, for instance, Zach Whalen's article "Play Along – An Approach to Videogame Music" which includes both).[[94]](#cite_note-94) The study of video game music is also known by some as "ludomusicology" – a portmanteau of "[ludology](/wiki/Ludology "Ludology")" (the study of games and gameplay) and "[musicology](/wiki/Musicology "Musicology")" (the study and analysis of music) – a term coined independently by Guillaume Laroche and Roger Moseley.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)

A prominent figure in early video game music and audio research is Karen Collins, who is associate professor at the [University of Waterloo](/wiki/University_of_Waterloo "University of Waterloo") and Canada Research Chair in Interactive Audio at the University of Waterloo Games Institute. Her monograph *Game Sound: An Introduction to the History, Theory and Practice of Video Game Music and Sound Design* (MIT Press 2008)[[97]](#cite_note-97) is considered a seminal work in the field, and was influential in the subsequent development of video game music studies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Ludomusicology Research Group is an inter-university research organisation focusing on the study of music in games, music games and music in video game culture, composed of four researchers: Michiel Kamp, Tim Summers, Melanie Fritsch, and Mark Sweeney.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Together they organise an annual international conference held in the UK or Europe (at the time of writing, the most recent was the Ludo2017 conference held at Bath Spa University).[[99]](#cite_note-99) The group was founded by Kamp, Summers and Sweeney in August 2011, who have also edited a collection of essays based around the study of game sound entitled *Ludomusicology: Approaches to Video Game Music*, published in July 2016.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) They also edited a double special issue of *The Soundtrack*[[102]](#cite_note-102) and initiated a new book series for the *Study in Game Sound and Music*[[103]](#cite_note-103) in 2017. In September 2016, Tim Summers' book 'Understanding Video Game Music' was published by Cambridge University Press.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)
Fritsch officially joined the group in 2016. She had edited the 2nd issue of the online journal *ACT – Zeitschrift für Musik und Performance*,[[106]](#cite_note-106) published in July 2011, which included ludomusicological contributions written by Tim Summers, Steven B. Reale and Jason Brame. She had been a regular at the conferences since 2012 and published several book chapters on the topic. Whereas Kamp, Summers and Sweeney have a background in [musicology](/wiki/Musicology "Musicology"), Fritsch's background is in [performance studies](/wiki/Performance_studies "Performance studies").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The North American Conference on Video Game Music (NACVGM) is an international conference on video game music held annually in North America since 2014.[[107]](#cite_note-107) It is organised by Neil Lerner, Steven Beverburg Reale and William Gibbons.[[108]](#cite_note-About_SSSMG-108)

In late 2016 the Society for the Study of Sound and Music in Games (SSSMG) was launched by the Ludomusicology Research Group in conjunction with the organisers of the North American Conference on Video Game Music and the Audio Mostly conference.[[108]](#cite_note-About_SSSMG-108) The SSSMG has the aim of bringing together both practitioners and researchers from across the globe in order to develop the field's understanding of sound and video game music and audio. Its focus is the use of its website as a "hub" for communication and resource centralisation, including a video game music research bibliography (a project initially begun by the Ludomusicology Research Group).[[109]](#cite_note-109)

The Ludomusicology Society of Australia was launched by Barnabas Smith in April 2017, during the Ludo2017 conference in Bath, UK; it aims to "offer a centralised and local professional body nurturing game music studies for academics, people in industry and game music fans alike in the Australasian region."[[110]](#cite_note-110)

## Composers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Composers")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may contain [original research](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit) by [verifying](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") the claims made and adding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources"). Statements consisting only of original research should be removed. *(August 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Creating and producing video game music requires strong teams and coordination among the different divisions of game development.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) As the market has expanded, so have the types of jobs in game music. The process often starts with the [game designer](/wiki/Game_designer "Game designer"), who will have a specific musical theme or genre in mind for the game.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Their options include contracting original composers or licensing existing music, both of which require other music experts.

During the arcade and early console era (1983 to the mid-1990s), most game music was composed by full-time employees of the particular game company producing the game. This was largely due to the very specialized nature of video game music, where each system had its own technology and tool sets. It was not uncommon for a game company like [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom") or [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami") to have a room full of composers, each at their own workstation with headphones writing music.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Once the CD-era hit and studio recorded music became more ubiquitous in games, it became increasingly common for game music to be composed by independent contractors, hired by the game developer on a per-project basis.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-twvideo01.ubm-us.net-113) Most bigger budget games such as *[Call of Duty](/wiki/Call_of_Duty "Call of Duty")*, *[Mass Effect](/wiki/Mass_Effect "Mass Effect")*, *[Ghost Recon](/wiki/Ghost_Recon "Ghost Recon")*, or *[Lost Planet](/wiki/Lost_Planet "Lost Planet")* hire composers in this fashion. Approximately 50% of game composers are freelance, the remaining being employees of a game company.[[114]](#cite_note-114) Original score and soundtrack may require the hiring of a [music director](/wiki/Music_director "Music director"), who will help create the game music as well as help book the resources needed for performing and recording the music.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Some music directors may work with a game's [Sound Designer](/wiki/Sound_Designer "Sound Designer") to create a dynamic score.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Notable exceptions include composer [Koji Kondo](/wiki/Koji_Kondo "Koji Kondo"), who remains an employee at Nintendo, and [Martin O'Donnell](/wiki/Martin_O%27Donnell "Martin O'Donnell"), who worked at Bungie until early 2014.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

The growth of casual, mobile and social games has greatly increased opportunities for game music composers, with job growth in the US market increasing more than 150% over five years.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Independently developed games are a frequent place where beginning game composers gain experience composing for video games.[[113]](#cite_note-twvideo01.ubm-us.net-113) Game composers, particularly for smaller games, are likely to provide other services such as sound design (76% of game composers also do some sound design), integration (47% of game composers also integrate their music into audio middleware), or even computer coding or scripting (15%).[[117]](#cite_note-117)

With the rising use of licensed popular music in video games, job opportunities in game music have also come to include the role of a [music supervisor](/wiki/Music_supervisor "Music supervisor"). Music supervisors work on behalf of a game developer or game publisher to source pre-existing music from artists and music publishers. These supervisors can be hired on a per-project basis or can work in-house, like the Music Group for [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") (EA) that has a team of music supervisors.[[118]](#cite_note-118) A music supervisor is needed to not only help select music that will suit the game, but to also ensure the music is fully licensed in order to avoid lawsuits or conflicts.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Music supervisors may also help negotiate payment, which for artists and songwriters is often a one-time buy-out fee, because games do not generate music royalties when they are sold.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) A growing trend is to contract artists to write original songs for games, to add to their value and exclusivity, and once again supervisors can be a part of that process.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Other")]

In 2011, video game music made its first appearance at the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") when "[Baba Yetu](/wiki/Baba_Yetu "Baba Yetu")", a song from *[Civilization IV](/wiki/Civilization_IV "Civilization IV")*, [won](/wiki/Grammy_Awards_of_2011#Composing_and_arranging "Grammy Awards of 2011") the [53rd annual music awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards_of_2011 "Grammy Awards of 2011")' [Best Instrumental Arrangement Accompanying Vocalists](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Instrumental_Arrangement_Accompanying_Vocalist(s) "Grammy Award for Best Instrumental Arrangement Accompanying Vocalist(s)"), making it the first video game music to be nominated for (or to win) a Grammy. The song won for its placement on [Christopher Tin](/wiki/Christopher_Tin "Christopher Tin")'s album *[Calling All Dawns](/wiki/Calling_All_Dawns "Calling All Dawns")*, but had been used in the game six years prior.[[133]](#cite_note-133) In 2022, video game music made another appearance at the Grammy Awards, with [The 8-Bit Big Band](/wiki/The_8-Bit_Big_Band "The 8-Bit Big Band")'s cover (arranged by [Charlie Rosen](/wiki/Charlie_Rosen_(musician) "Charlie Rosen (musician)") and Jake Silverman) of "Meta Knight's Revenge", a song from [Kirby Super Star](/wiki/Kirby_Super_Star "Kirby Super Star"), [winning](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards#Music_for_Visual_Media "64th Annual Grammy Awards") the [64th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "64th Annual Grammy Awards")' Grammy Award for Best Arrangement, Instrumental or A Capella.[[134]](#cite_note-Kotaku-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)

Other video game awards include the [International Film Music Critics Association](/wiki/International_Film_Music_Critics_Association "International Film Music Critics Association") (IFMCA) award for Best Original Score for Interactive Media and [Machinima.com](/wiki/Machinima.com "Machinima.com")'s [Inside Gaming](/wiki/Inside_Gaming "Inside Gaming") Awards for Best Original Score and Best Sound Design.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In addition to recognizing the composers of original score, the Guild of Music Supervisors offer a GMS Award to the music supervisors that select and coordinate licensed music for video games.[[136]](#cite_note-:0-136)

## Fan culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_music&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Fan culture")]

Video game fans have created their own fan sites "dedicated to the appreciation and promotion of video game music", such as [OverClocked ReMix](/wiki/OverClocked_ReMix "OverClocked ReMix").[[137]](#cite_note-137) There are also [YouTube channels](/wiki/YouTube_channels "YouTube channels") dedicated to the discussion and analysis of video game music, such as Visualizer Music Tracks and 8-Bit Music Theory.

Fans also make their own song remixes and compilations, like insaneintherainmusic, and have built online remixing communities through the ease of internet distribution.[[4]](#cite_note-collins_12-4)

The Japanese [dōjin music](/wiki/D%C5%8Djin_music "Dōjin music") scene is notable for producing albums of arranged videogame music which derived from popular retro franchises such as *[Mega Man](/wiki/Mega_Man "Mega Man")*, *[Chrono Trigger](/wiki/Chrono_Trigger "Chrono Trigger")* or *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*,[[138]](#cite_note-138) from [dōjin games](/wiki/D%C5%8Djin_soft "Dōjin soft"), such as *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")*, studio [Key](/wiki/Key_(company) "Key (company)") visual novels, and the *[When They Cry](/wiki/When_They_Cry_(disambiguation) "When They Cry (disambiguation)")* series, and from popular franchises on [Comiket](/wiki/Comiket "Comiket"), such as [Type-Moon](/wiki/Type-Moon "Type-Moon")'s *Fate* series or *[Kantai Collection](/wiki/Kantai_Collection "Kantai Collection")*.[[139]](#cite_note-139) There have been over six thousand dōjin albums of *Touhou Project* music released.[[140]](#cite_note-140)
